## SUMMARY OF ANALYSIS

My idea was that the course should operate at a slightly faster pace with lectures moving a bit faster and going through more practice problems and examples because more practice will benefit student learning and faster pace will help to keep students focused. 




## I concluded....
### Part 1.5: Conclusion

Based on my analysis, it does not seem like there is a strong relationship between the perceived course pace and student self rated understanding. As you can see in one of the first graphs created that looked at course pace vs understanding, there seemed to be a slight negative relationship as students who believed the pace was fast, ranked understanding lower than students who believed the pace was slow or in the middle range. In the second graph, you can observe pace vs. difficulty. In this graph, it appears that there is a strong positive relationship between perceived pace and student difficulty. Intuitively, these results make sense. It makes sense that students who perceived the pace as too fast would struggle with the materials and concepts, and that students who perceived the pace as very slow would feel comfortable with the concepts. However, none of this data suggests a strong consistent relationship betwen perceived course pace and student understanding. When looking at how high-understanding students ranked pace, most reported a moderate to slightly fast pace, but understanding levels appear to remain relatively high and consistent across pace categories. This suggests that these variations in pace perception do not dramatically predict differences in understanding.

When looking at practice based components like programming assignments, LSQs, and quizzes, there is a more noticeable pattern. When looking at all students, there appears to be a positive relationship between understanding and and effectiveness. However for LSQs specifically, the data appears to be relatively consistent across student rankings, suggesting a weaker relationship.When looking at 243 high understanding students (>= 5) they tended to rank these types of practice exercises as highly effective. However, this relationship is not linear and varies across different types of practice. 

Overall, although the results suggest that practice-based activities contribute to improved understanding, course pace does not appear to be a predictor of student success. Learning outcomes are influenced by many factors, as you can tell by the many different variables in our CSV sets. 

This data inconclusive. Based on this data, I would not recommend major changes to course pace since the relationship with understanding is weak and inconsistent. My idea was that course pace should increase to make time for more practice exercises. Although the data showed that high performing students (understanding >= 5) thought practice exercises were effective, this data however showed no strong or consistent relationship between perceived pace and student understanding. There was a small trend that a faster perceived pace was related to lower understanding, but this wasn't consistent across all groups, there is also a stronger relationship between pace and difficulty, showing that faster perceived pace was associated with higher difficulty. Additionally, because this data is based on self-reported perceptions and not actual grades, its difficult to validate the results of this analysis. The relationships shown are weak an inconsistent.  

Increasing course pace could increase stress for students and reduce accessibility for beginning programmers. Additionally, increasing the practice workload could improve learning but increase student workload and increase the grading load for instructional staff. To get more data for this idea, it would be helpful to gather objective performance data and measure time and effort spent on assignments and practice activities. Additionally, instructional staff could run an experimental section with an adjusted pace and added practice to compare outcomes.
